ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for winter melon soup specifically; mapped to the closest parent class 'soup food product' (FOODON_03311737). A more specific term for Chinese winter melon soup is not present in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for winter melon soup in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for winter melon soup as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Winter Melon Soup is approximately 300 g (one bowl), providing roughly 135 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 6.6 g fat, and 8.4 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Pork rib soup with winter melon — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for winter melon (wax gourd, cooked) and pork ribs (cooked, simmered)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the composite dish 'Winter Melon Soup'. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for wax gourd / winter melon cooked (FDC ID 169228) and pork ribs simmered, weighted to reflect a typical recipe composition of approximately 60% winter melon, 25% pork rib meat and bone broth contribution, and 15% water/seasoning. Sodium reflects added salt typical of home preparation. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these USDA anchors and adjusted where discrepancies exceeded 15%.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on wax gourd / winter melon and clear meat-based soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Winter Melon Soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on: (1) wax gourd / winter melon has negligible starch and very high water content, analogous to other low-GI non-starchy vegetables (GI ~15–30 per Sydney GI Database); (2) the pork rib broth contributes protein and fat which further blunt glycaemic response; (3) overall carbohydrate content of 2.8 g/100 g is very low. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 8.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.1 g, classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 180 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 540 mg sodium, which is about 24% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one bowl and request reduced salt during preparation, as the sodium from added salt and pork rib broth can accumulate significantly; the potassium content (150 mg/100 g) provides a partial counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g/100 g) and estimated low glycemic index (~25) result in a glycemic load of only 2.1 per standard 300 g serving, posing minimal postprandial glucose impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Winter Melon Soup is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load and high water content; it can be consumed freely as a side dish provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs (estimated 0.8 g/100 g) and sodium (180 mg/100 g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; however, the overall fat and calorie load is low relative to most meat-based dishes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Winter Melon Soup in moderate portions, preferring lean pork or chicken variants to reduce saturated fat, and requesting low-salt preparation to keep sodium within daily cardiovascular targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger and winter melon provide antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory compounds; the soup is light and easy to digest.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Winter Melon Soup is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a light, anti-inflammatory side dish; no specific cautions apply beyond general dietary balance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Winter Melon Soup (冬瓜汤 / 冬瓜湯, Dōngguātāng) is a classic Chinese and Cantonese clear soup prepared by simmering large chunks of winter melon (wax gourd, Benincasa hispida) in a savoury broth made from pork ribs or chicken. The winter melon becomes soft and translucent during cooking, absorbing the flavours of the broth while contributing a mild, slightly sweet taste and a delicate, silky texture. Aromatics such as fresh ginger are commonly added, and premium versions include dried scallops (conpoy) or red dates (jujube) for additional depth and sweetness. The dish is low in calories (approximately 45 kcal per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (2.8 g/100 g), and provides moderate protein (3.5 g/100 g) from the meat and broth. It has an estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 (low) and a glycemic load of only 2.1 per standard 300 g serving, making it highly suitable for individuals managing blood sugar. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, winter melon is prized for its 'cooling' (yin) properties and is frequently consumed during hot weather or after rich, 'heaty' meals to restore balance. Clinically, the soup is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes and weight management due to its low energy density and negligible glycaemic impact; sodium should be monitored for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is a staple at Chinese family meals, typically served as a side soup alongside rice and other dishes at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish Winter Melon Soup. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for wax gourd (cooked) and pork ribs (simmered), weighted by typical recipe composition. The GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable low-carbohydrate non-starchy vegetable soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (wax gourd cooked, FDC ID 169228; pork ribs simmered) weighted at approximately 60% winter melon, 25% pork contribution, 15% water/seasoning. No exact single-entry match exists in any approved source for this composite dish. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: winter melon contains negligible beta-carotene and the pork rib broth contributes no meaningful vitamin A; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central wax gourd entry. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero in this minimally processed home-cooked soup with no hydrogenated fats. 4. saturated_fat_g (0.8 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g), cholesterol_mg (12.0 mg), and water_g (90.0 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork rib and wax gourd entries; these fields were set to -1 in the input and have been populated with USDA-anchored estimates. 5. The input source (Guangzhou University of Chinese Medicine) is not on the approved source list and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence base. 6. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 300 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 300 g default serving. 8. This dish is Chinese / Cantonese in origin and is not classified as a Singaporean dish; therefore the Singaporean multilingual requirements (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided as mandatory for a Chinese-origin dish.
